CHAPTER 67
(H. B. 265 )
AN ACT concerning the annual levy of taxes by cities of the first class .
the disposition of revenues, and the effect of the failure to make such
a levy .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 91.280 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended and as so amended shall read :
( 1 ) The board of aldermen , in its annual levy of taxes ,
shall make such separate levies as are required by law or as
the board deems necessary or desirable , and a general levy in
such amount as in its judgment is necessary and advisable .
The amounts levied shall be collected and paid to the city , the
amount of each separate levy being carried to the credit of
the agency for which levied , and the amount of the general
levy being credited to the general fund of the city . The amount
of any separate levy to be credited to any agency shall not be
diverted from it , or used for any purpose other than that
specified by the levy , but shall remain as a separate fund in
the hands of the director of finance .

AN ACT relating to assessment returns in cities of the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-

Section 1. Section 91.350 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended and reenacted and as so amended and reen-
acted shall read as follows :
The assessor shall , on or before September 10th of each

property .

CHAPTER 69
(H. B. 267 )
AN ACT concerning the Tax Receiver in cities of the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 91.110 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended and reenacted and as so amended and reen-
acted shall read :
(1 ) The director of finance shall appoint a tax receiver ,
who shall hold office until he resigns or is removed . The direc-
tor of finance may , subject to the approval of the mayor , re-
move the tax receiver at any time .
( 2 ) The tax receiver shall collect all city taxes except
those that are to be collected by the sinking fund , and shall
make a settlement with the director of finance daily . He shall
each day make out a statement showing his collection of taxes
for the previous day and shall deliver one copy thereof to the
director of finance and one to the comptroller and inspector .
He shall keep the books and accounts of his office in such
manner as is prescribed by ordinance or , in the absence of any
such ordinance , by the director of finance , and shall perform
any other duties imposed by law .
( 3 ) The tax receiver shall be charged with all uncollected
bills and shall account for the same in such manner as is pre-
scribed by ordinance , or , in the absence of any such ordinance ,
by the director of finance. No tax receiver shall be eligible to
any office unless he has accounted to the city for all collections
and obtained his quietus .
( 4) The tax receiver may appoint , with the approval of
the director of finance , such deputies as are allowed him by
ordinance . He shall give bond for the faithful performance of
his duties , to be approved by the mayor and the board of
aldermen , and the bond shall include responsibility for the
acts of his deputies .
(5) The tax receiver and his deputies shall be paid annual
salaries , to be fixed by ordinance .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

CHAPTER 70
(H. B. 268 )
AN ACT concerning accounts and records kept by the director of finance ;
duties as to claims against city ; countersigning of checks , in cities of
the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 91.050 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended and reenacted and as so amended and reen-
acted shall read :
( 1 ) The director of finance shall adopt a proper method
of bookkeeping and shall preserve in his office all accounts ,
vouchers , documents and papers relating to the accounts , con-
tracts , debts , revenues and other fiscal affairs of the city . He
shall keep records showing the financial operation and condi-
tion of the city , its assets and liabilities , the expenditures au-
thorized , and all contracts in which the city is interested , with
the names of the contractors . He shall state and render all
accounts between the city and any other person , except when
otherwise provided by law .
( 2 ) The director of finance shall examine , adjust and
audit all unsettled claims against the city , for the payment of
which any money may be drawn from the city treasury , and
after examining the claim and all accompanying vouchers and
documents , shall approve the balance due on the claim . No
such claim or any part thereof shall be audited against the
city unless it is authorized by law or ordinance and is in
proper and fully itemized form , and unless the amount re-
quired for its payment has been appropriated for that pur-
pose by the board of aldermen . The director of finance may
require a statement of accounts to be verified by affidavit .
(3) The director of finance shall countersign all checks
on the city treasury , and record the amount and nature of
each .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

(H. B. 269)
AN ACT concerning the receipt , custody and disbursement of city funds ;
receipts ; in cities of the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 91.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended and reenacted and as so amended and reen-
acted shall read :
The Director of Finance shall receive and keep all money
of the city and pay it out on checks signed by the accountant
and countersigned by the director of finance , or , in the lat-
ter's absence , by the mayor . All money belonging to the City
received by a city officer or agent from any source shall be
deposited in the city treasury through the department of pub-
lic finance regularly once a day , unless otherwise provided by
statute or ordinance . The director of finance shall make out
receipts , at least in triplicate , setting out the amount paid ,
the source of the payment , and to what accounts credited , and
shall give one to the person paying and one to the comptroller
and inspector.
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

CHAPTER 74
(H. B. 272 )
AN ACT relating to the methods , apparatus and standards to be used in
grading wheat .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 363.060 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
(1 ) Any person buying wheat by grade shall follow the
following procedure and use the following standard apparatus
for determining the test weight per bushel :
(a ) Use an accurate quart or one-half gallon size weight-
per -bushel testing apparatus , the kettle of which must have
a capacity of exactly 67.2 and 134.4 cubic inches respectively .
(b) A round hopper shall be above the quart or one- half
gallon size kettle and the opening at the bottom shall be ex-
actly 1 1/4 inches in diameter .
(c) The bottom of the hopper must be held exactly 2
inches above the center of the kettle .
(d ) A stroker made of hard wood , with smooth rounded
edges , 12 inches long , % inches thick , and 1 % inches broad
must be used .

CHAPTER 75
(H. B. 276 )
AN ACT to repeal , amend and re- enact Section 95.580 , Kentucky Revised
Statutes , relating to the policemen's and firemen's pension funds in
cities of the second and third class and fixing rate of tax and assess-
ment and designating the source of said fund .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
That Section 95.580 , Kentucky Revised Statutes , be re-
pealed , amended and re -enacted so that the same shall read
as follows :
( 1 ) The policemen's and firemen's pension fund in cities
of the second class shall consist of :
(a ) The proceeds of an annual tax , which may be levied
by the city legislative body , of not less than two cents nor
more than eight cents on each one hundred dollars in value of
taxable property .

CHAPTER 77
(H. B. 282)
AN ACT to prohibit the sale , purchase , barter , exchange or trade of wild
rabbits ; to prohibit the possession of wild rabbits for sale , barter , ex-
change or trade and to prohibit an attempt to sell , purchase , barter ,
exchange or trade wild rabbits .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. As used in this act unless the context requires
otherwise :
( 1 ) " Buy " and " sell " have the meaning given to them
in KRS 150.010 .
( 2 ) " Rabbit " means , any wild rabbit ( including cotton-
tail , jack -rabbit , and swamp rabbit ) , whether alive or dead
or any part thereof whether taken within or without the state .
Section 2. No person shall buy or sell or attempt to buy
or sell a rabbit .
Section 3. Subsections ( 17 ) and ( 18 ) of section 150.175
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are hereby repealed .
Section 4. Subsection ( 2 ) of section 150.180 of the Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows :
"Mussels and fish , other than black bass ( large mouth or
small mouth ) crappie , jack salmon , or wall -eyed pike or
striped bass , sand pike or sauger and muskellunge may be
bought and sold , and transported , if in legal possession . ”
Section 5. Subparagraphs ( q ) and ( r ) of subsection ( 2 )
of section 150.220 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are here-
by repealed .

CHAPTER 78
(H. B. 283 )
AN ACT relating to game and fish and the Division of Game and Fish .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. The declared purpose of this Act and the
policy of the Commonwealth of Kentucky is to protect and
conserve the wildlife of this Commonwealth so as to insure
a permanent and continued supply of the wildlife resources
of this state for the purpose of furnishing sport and recrea-
tion for the present and future residents of this state ; to
promote the general welfare of this Commonwealth ; to pro-
vide for the prudent taking and disposing of wildlife within
reasonable limits based upon the adequacy of the supply
thereof ; to protect the food supply of this state , and to insure
the long-term continuance of an important part of the com-
merce of this state which depends upon the existence of the
wildlife resources . It is the declared purpose of this Act and
the policy of this Commonwealth that an adequate and flexible
system be installed to accomplish the aforesaid purposes . The
provisions of this Act shall be liberally construed to carry out
the purposes and intentions of this Act.
Section 2. Section 150.010 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :

"As used in this Chapter , unless the context otherwise
requires :
" (1) ' Angling ' means taking fish by hook and line in
hand , rod in hand , set line or trot line , but does not include
the use of grappling hooks , gang hooks , or snag lines , or gigs
and nets of any kind .
(2) ' Buy ' includes offering to buy , acquiring or possess-
ing through purchase , barter , exchange or trade.
(3) ' Commission ' means the Game and Fish Commis-
sion .
(4) ' Device ' means any article , instrument or equip-
ment of whatever nature or kind which may be used to take
wild animals , wild birds or fish .
(5) ' Director ' means the Director of the Division of
Game and Fish .
(6) 'Division ' means the Division of Game and Fish .
(7) ' Impounded waters ' means any public waters
backed up by a dam constructed and used for hydro - electric
purposes or for the purposes of flood control .
(8) 'Minnows ' means all fish under six inches in length ,
except bass (large and small mouth ) , rock bass , goggle -eye ,
trout , crappie , jack salmon , walleyed pike or striped bass ,
sand pike or sauger or muskellunge .
(9) ' Navigable waters ' means any waters within this
state under lock and dam .
(10 ) ' Nonresident ' is a person who has not resided in
this state for one year prior to his application for a license .
(11 ) ' Public waters ' means all waters within the state
flowing in a natural channel stream .
( 12 ) ' Raw fur ' means a hide , fur or pelt of a fur -bear-
ing animal which has not been processed by an established
commercial processor . Skinning , stretching , oiling or coloring
by the person who took the animal shall not be considered as
processing .
(13 ) ' Resident ' is a person who has resided in this
state for one year prior to his application for a license .

this Act relating to wildlife and exercise all necessary powers
incident thereto ."

classification.title: Amendment to Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 150.021
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 150.021 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, which establishes the Division of Game and Fish as an independent state agency. It outlines the composition of the division (Game and Fish Commission, director, conservation officers, and other employees) and grants it the power to enforce laws and regulations related to wildlife. The statute also includes definitions for terms like 'sell', 'take', 'transport', 'wild bird', 'wild animal', 'wildlife', 'regulation', and 'prescribed by the division'. Section 4 discusses conditions under which the director and commission can act if wildlife populations become depleted.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an amendment to a section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the establishment and powers of the Division of Game and Fish. It focuses on wildlife management, definitions of terms related to hunting and fishing, and the administrative structure of the wildlife agency. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 150.120 regarding conservation regulations
classification.summary: This statute amends Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) 150.120, concerning the adoption, filing, and effectiveness of regulations by the director of conservation. It outlines procedures for filing regulations with county court clerks, handling emergency regulations, and the effective date of regulations based on their filing with the Secretary of State. It also states that lack of actual notice does not excuse violations. The amendment also modifies subsections (1), (2), and (5) of KRS 150.120, specifically detailing the seizure powers of conservation officers and peace officers regarding various items related to hunting and fishing.

classification.title: Amendment to Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 150.180 concerning fur buyers
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 150.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, which governs the buying, selling, and transportation of wild animals, wild birds, fish, and raw furs. It clarifies licensing requirements for resident and non-resident fur buyers and processors, and outlines conditions for transporting furs within and out of the state. The amendments specify that non-residents need a license to buy furs from within the state and ship them out, while residents can transport furs within the state. It also allows individuals who have lawfully taken or purchased pelts to ship them to licensed non-resident fur processors or taxidermists.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with regulations for fur buyers and the trade of raw furs. It specifies licensing requirements and rules for transporting furs within and out of the state. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be purely regulatory concerning the fur trade. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws, with a high confidence. The category is 'other' as it does not fit into the specific categories provided. The document type is 'codes' because it explicitly refers to amending the 'Kentucky Revised Statutes'.

classification.title: An Act relating to game and fish and amending Section 150.280 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
classification.summary: This act details the fee structure for licenses and tags related to game and fish, assents to federal laws encouraging cooperation in fish restoration and management projects, and outlines the process for issuing permits to propagate fish, frogs, and fur-bearing animals. It specifies that funds from fishermen's license fees shall not be diverted from the administration of the Division of Game and Fish. It also allows for permits to be issued without fees to fish and game clubs cooperating with the division.

commissioned officer or patrolman , who possesses the powers
of a peace officer ; ( d ) " Civilian " shall mean such experts ,
statisticians , clerks and other assistants who do not possess
the powers of a peace officer .

istrative body of the State Government within the meaning
of Chapter 12 KRS which shall be known and is hereby
designated as the Kentucky State Police . The head of the

ed by the Governor and who shall hold office at the pleasure

ence and qualification for this position and preferably shall

CHAPTER 80 181
the enforcement of the judgment of the trial board upon said
charges shall not be suspended during said appeal .
To perfect said appeal within the time specified , such of-
ficer shall file in the office of the Clerk of the Franklin Circuit
Court a copy of the order , of all the evidence heard and of all
the steps taken by the trial board relative to such charges , but
shall first post a bond to secure the cost of the action in a
lump sum to be approved by the Circuit Clerk , with corporate
surety approved by the Division of Insurance as to solvency
and responsibility and authorized to transact business in this
State , or he may post a cash bond . The members of the trial
board and the Commissioner shall be necessary parties to such
appeal . The Circuit Clerk shall docket the case as though it
were a petition in equity and shall immediately issue a sum-
mons for the appellee . The summons shall be returnable in the
same manner as in equity cases . Service of summons upon the
Commissioner or acting Commissioner shall be deemed service
upon the board.
Such action shall be set down for trial as soon as possible ,
and the hearing thereof shall be expedited in the same man-
ner as a declaratory judgment suit .
No new or additional evidence shall be introduced in the
Franklin Circuit Court , except as to fraud or misconduct of
some party engaged in the administration of this Act , or one
who is a member of the trial board , but the court shall other-
wise hear the case upon the record as attested by the board ,
and in all respects dispose of the appeal in a summary man-
ner . Its review shall be limited to determining whether or not
(a ) the board acted without or in excess of its powers ; (b ) the
order appealed from was procured by fraud ; ( c ) if questions
of fact are in issue , whether or not any substantial evidence
supports the order appealed from . After such a hearing , the
court shall enter a judgment sustaining or setting aside the
order of the trial board appealed from . The cost of the action
shall follow the judgment of the court .
Any party aggrieved by a judgment of the Franklin Cir-
cuit Court may appeal to the Court of Appeals in the same
manner that appeals are taken under Section 639a -5 of the
Civil Code of Practice .

CHAPTER 84
(H. B. 304)
AN ACT repealing and reenacting Section 70.030 Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes relating to deputy sheriffs and their salaries .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
That Section 70.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes be ,
and the same is , hereby repealed and reenacted so that as re-
enacted said Section shall read as follows :
(1 ) Except as provided in subsections ( 2 ) and ( 3 ) , the
sheriff may , with the approval of the county court, appoint
his own deputies , and may revoke the appointment at his
pleasure . Before any deputy executes the duties of his office ,
he shall take the oath required to be taken by the sheriff .
(2 ) The sheriff of each county having an assessed value
of more than $100,000,000 containing a population of less than
75,000 , comprising a separate judicial district , and containing
a second class city , may appoint such deputies and clerical
assistants , subject to the approval of the fiscal court, as are
necessary for the proper performance of the duties of his of-
fice . The sheriff with the approval of the fiscal court shall fix
the salaries of the deputies and clerical assistants , but the
salaries of all the deputies and clerical assistants , shall not
exceed $ 25,000 in any year . The fiscal court shall pay the an-
nual salaries of the deputies and clerical assistants in monthly
installments out of the county treasury .

198 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 92
(H. B. 352 )
AN ACT amending sub - section ( 5 ) of Section 132.590 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes providing for compensation of County tax com-
missioner and deputies.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of Kentucky that
sub -section ( 5 ) of section 132.590 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes be amended to read as follows :
( 5 ) In counties in which the assessed value of property
exceeds fifteen million dollars , the county tax commissioner
shall be allowed by the state as compensation to his deputies
two cents on each one hundred dollars of the property assessed
in excess of fifteen million dollars , but the total sum allowed
to any county tax commissioner as deputy hire shall not ex-
ceed seven thousand five hundred dollars in any county except
in a county having a city of the first class , which shall be
governed by KRS 132.630 . In any county assessing more than
one hundred and twenty -five million dollars in taxable prop-
erty , the county shall allow and pay to the county tax commis-
sioner from its funds , for assistants , six thousand dollars . In
any county assessing more than eighty million dollars in tax-
able property , the county shall allow and pay to the county
tax commissioner from its funds , for assistants , three thou-
sand dollars . In any county assessing more than thirty-five
million dollars in taxable property , the county shall allow and
pay to the county tax commissioner from its funds , for as-
sistants , one thousand five hundred dollars . In counties assess-
ing less than $35,000,000 in taxable property , the county shall
allow and pay to the county tax commissioner from its funds ,
for assistants , $200 .
Not Approved .
Received by Secretary of State March 30 , 1948 .
CHAPTER 93
(H. B. 353 )
AN ACT relating to Revenue and Taxation .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :

classification.summary: This statute amends Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) Chapter 141, specifically sections 141.060 and 141.100. Section 141.060, as amended, pertains to deductions for medical care expenses from adjusted gross income, with maximum deduction limits based on the number of exemptions allowed. Section 141.100, also amended, details the treatment of gains and losses from the sale or disposition of property for tax purposes, distinguishing between property held for less than two years and property held for two years or more, with exceptions for inventory and property held for sale in the regular course of business. It also addresses property exchanges and corporate reorganizations.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses tax regulations related to medical expense deductions and gains/losses from property sales. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general tax law amendment. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to Kentucky Revised Statutes Sections 140.240 and 140.030
classification.summary: This statute amends two sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes related to estate and inheritance taxes. Section 140.240 is amended to clarify procedures for estimating and paying taxes on estates, including provisions for discounts, interest, penalties, and refunds. Section 140.030(2) is amended to specify that proceeds from life insurance policies are taxable as part of a legacy, with an exemption for up to twenty thousand dollars payable to a designated beneficiary, excluding policies issued by the U.S. Government. The amendment also clarifies the taxability of U.S. Government life insurance policies.

classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) 138.360 and 138.390 concerning the taxation of motor fuels other than gasoline. It establishes an excise tax of seven cents per gallon on the use of such fuel in internal combustion engines for motor vehicles on public highways. Two-sevenths of the collected tax revenue is to be allocated by the Department of Highways specifically for the construction, reconstruction, and maintenance of rural and secondary roads.

classification.title: AN ACT to repeal, amend and re-enact Section 138.500 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes relating to Revenue and Taxation.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 138.500 to repeal and reenact provisions related to revenue and taxation. Specifically, it states that no suit shall be maintained in any court to restrain or delay the collection of taxes imposed by KRS 138.020, 138.140, 138.220, 138.360, or 138.460. Aggrieved taxpayers must pay the tax under protest as and when due and may seek a refund within two years from the date of payment. The act also includes provisions for monthly reporting of fuel usage and tax computation, with an effective date of April 1, 1948, to align with the fiscal year for the Department of Highways.

CHAPTER 102
(H. B. 374)
AN ACT relating to the establishment of flood control systems in cities
of the fourth class , providing the manner in which such systems
may be established and financed .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
SECTION 1. As used in this Act the words " govern-
ing body " shall mean and include the Board of Councilmen ,
Board of Aldermen and Board of Commissioners or other
legislative body of a city of the fourth class .
SECTION 2. All cities of the fourth class are hereby
empowered to establish a flood control system for the pro-
tection or reclamation of property , situated within the limits
of such city, from floods or high waters and to protect prop-
erty in such city from the effects of water for sanitary or
other purposes , whenever the establishment of such a flood
control system shall , in the judgment of the governing
authority of such city , be conducive to the public health , con-
venience and welfare : for the purpose of establishing such
a flood control system any such city may acquire , construct ,
equip , maintain and operate walls and other barriers , drains ,
ditches , levees and other works proper to that purpose and
may enlarge , extend , straighten , deepen , widen or otherwise
improve any such structure or work .
For this purpose any such city is empowered to acquire
any and all land or any interest therein , rights of way or
other property , by gift , purchase or by condemnation , wheth-
er within or without the corporate limits of such city , and to
establish , construct , operate and maintain such a flood con-
trol system either within or without the limits of such city .
The establishment , construction and operation of a flood
control system , as authorized by this Act , is declared to be a

classification.title: An Act relating to flood control systems and assessments
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for cities to establish and fund flood control systems. It details how assessments for these systems are to be levied on real property within a benefited area, based on assessed value for city taxation. The act specifies that no real property, except that owned by the U.S. Government or the Commonwealth of Kentucky, shall be exempt from these assessments. It also establishes a lien superior to most other liens for these assessments and provides for foreclosure actions in equity to collect delinquent assessments.

CHAPTER 103 243
CHAPTER 103
AN ACT concerning armories .
(H. B. 377 )
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
SECTION I. There is hereby appropriated to the De-
partment of Military Affairs out of the General Expenditure
Fund for each of the fiscal years ending June 30 , 1949 , and
June 30 , 1950 , the sum of one hundred thousand dollars
($ 100,000 ) for the purpose of constructing , reconstructing ,
acquiring , maintaining , renting , supplying , equipping , in-
suring and providing armories for the use of the Armories
Division .
SECTION II . Due to the uncertainty of obtaining ma-
terials , equipment and labor at the time of the passage of
this Act, it is the intention of the General Assembly that any
unexpended balance of the appropriation of one hundred
thousand dollars ( $ 100,000 ) for the fiscal year ending June
30 , 1949 , made by Section I of this Act shall not revert to
the General Expenditure Fund , but shall continue to the next
fiscal year ending June 30 , 1950 .
SECTION III . KRS 36.080 ( 3 ) shall not apply to
armories constructed by or rented from the Commonwealth
of Kentucky Military Department Armory Corporation ,
organized under the laws of Kentucky July 10 , 1939 .
SECTION IV . Nothing in this Act shall be construed to
repeal any appropriation to the Department of Military
Affairs for the fiscal year ending June 30 , 1948. The ap-
propriation made by Section I of this Act shall not replace
any other appropriation to the Department of Military
Affairs for the fiscal biennium ending June 30 , 1950 , which
may be passed by this session of the General Assembly , but
shall be in addition to all sums heretofore or that may be
hereafter appropriated to the Department of Military Af-
fairs for other purposes .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
1. Section 100.068 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

The lawful use of land for trade , industry or residence ,
existing at the time of adoption of any zoning regulation or

of , or amendment thereto , although such use does not conform
to the provisions of such new regulations or restrictions , may

is amended to read as follows :
The use of a building or structure existing at the time

at the time of any adjustment or revision thereof or amend-
ment thereto , although such use does not conform to the
provisions of such new regulations or restrictions , may be
continued , and a noncorforming use of a building or structure
may be changed to another nonconforming use of the same
or more restricted classification .
§ 3. Section 100.071 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is repealed .
Not Approved .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1- PURPOSE OF ACT
The purpose of this Act is to promote the public welfare
by regulating insurance rates to the end that they shall not
be excessive , inadequate or unfairly discriminatory , and to
authorize and regulate cooperative action among insurers in
rate making and in other matters within the scope of this
Act . Nothing in this Act is intended ( 1 ) to prohibit or dis-
courage reasonable competition , or ( 2 ) to prohibit , or en-
courage except to the extent necessary to accomplish the
aforementioned purpose , uniformity in insurance rates ,
rating systems , rating plans or practices . This Act shall be
liberally interpreted to carry into effect the provisions of
this Section .
Sec . 2- SCOPE OF ACT
(1 ) This Act applies to all kinds , types or forms of fire ,
marine, inland marine insurance and allied lines or risks
located in this state . Inland marine insurance shall be deem-
ed to include insurance now or hereafter defined by statute ,
or by interpretation thereof , or if not so defined or interpret-
ed , by ruling of the Director of insurance , hereinafter re-
ferred to as Director , or as established by general custom of
the business , as inland marine insurance . With respect to
such kinds , types or forms of insurance , this Act applies to
inter -insurers or reciprocal underwriters regulated by Sec-
tions 302.080 to 302.140 , inclusive of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes , and mutual insurers regulated by Sections 301.010
to 301.140 , inclusive , of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , and
insurers operating on the Lloyd's Plan , regulated by Sec-
tions 302.150 to 302.230 , inclusive , of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes .

be granted or denied in whole or in part by the Director
within sixty days of the date of its filing with him . Licenses
issued pursuant to this Section shall remain in effect for
three years unless sooner suspended or revoked by the Di-

CHAPTER 105 257
Sec . 12- EXAMINATIONS
The Director shall, at least once in five years , make or
cause to be made an examination of each rating organization

made an examination of each advisory organization referred

any such examination shall be paid by the rating organization ,
advisory organization , or group , association or other organ-
ization examined upon presentation to it of a detailed ac-
count of such costs . The officers , manager , agents and em-
ployees of such rating organizations , advisory organization ,
or group , association or other organization may be examined
at any time under oath and shall exhibit all books , records ,
accounts , documents , or agreements governing its method of
operation . The Director shall furnish two copies of the ex-
amination report to the organization , group or association
examined and shall notify such organization , group or
association that it may, within twenty days thereafter, re-
quest a hearing on said report or on any facts or recommenda-
tions therein . Before filing any such report for public in-
spection , the Director shall grant a hearing to the organiza-
tion , group or association examined . The report of any such
examination , when filed for public inspection , shall be ad-
missable in evidence in any action or proceeding brought by
the Director against the organization , group or associa-
tion examined , or its officers or agents , and shall be prima
facie evidence of the facts stated therein . The Director may
withhold the report of any such examination from public in-
spection for such time as he may deem proper . In lieu of any
such examination the Director may accept the report of an
examination made by the insurance supervisory official of
another state , pursuant to the laws of such state .

CHAPTER 105 259
Sec . 15.-FALSE AND MISLEADING INFORMATION
No person or organization shall willfully withhold in-
formation from , or knowingly give false or misleading in-
formation to , the Director , any statistical agency designated
by the Director , any rating organization , or any insurer ,
which will affect the rates or premiums chargeable under
this Act. A violation of this Section shall subject the one
guilty of such violation to the penalties provided in Sections
17 and 18 of this Act .
Sec . 16.- REBATES PROHIBITED
No insurer or employee thereof , and no agent shall
knowingly charge , demand or receive a premium for any
policy of insurance except in accordance with the applicable
filing on file with the Director . No insurer or employee
thereof, and no agent shall pay, allow , or give , or offer to
pay , allow , or give , directly or indirectly , as an inducement
to insurance , or after insurance has been effected , any re-
bate , discount, abatement , credit or reduction of the premium
named in a policy of insurance , or any special favor or ad-
vantage in the dividends or other benefits to accrue thereon ,
or any valuable consideration or inducement whatever , not
specified in the policy of insurance , except to the extent
provided for in such applicable filing . No insured named in
a policy of insurance , nor any employee of such insured shall
knowingly receive or accept , directly or indirectly , any such
rebate , discount , abatement or reduction of premium, or any
special favor or advantage or valuable consideration or in-
ducement . Nothing herein contained shall be construed as
prohibiting the payment of commissions or other compensa-
tion to duly licensed agents and brokers , nor as prohibiting
any participating insurer from distributing to its policy-
holders dividends , savings or the unused or unabsorbed por-
tion of premiums and premium deposits .
Sec . 17.- PENALTIES .
Any person or organization violating any provision of
this Act shall upon conviction , be fined not more than fifty
dollars ( $ 50 ) for each such violation , but if such violation is

Sec . 18.- SUSPENSION OF LICENSE
The Director may suspend the license of any rating
organization or insurer which fails to comply with an order of
the Director within the time limited by such order , or any
extension thereof which the Director may grant . The Di-
rector shall not suspend the license of any rating organiza-
tion or insurer for failure to comply with an order until the
time prescribed for an appeal therefrom has expired or if an
appeal has been taken , until such order has been affirmed .
The Director may determine when a suspension of license
shall become effective and it shall remain in effect for the
period fixed by him, unless he modifies or rescinds such
suspension , or until the order upon which such suspension is
based is modified , rescinded or reversed . No license shall
be suspended or revoked except upon a written order of the
Director , stating his findings , made after a hearing held
upon not less than ten days ' written notice to such person
or organization specifying the alleged violation .
Sec . 19.-HEARINGS , PROCEDURE
(1 ) Any insurer or rating organization aggrieved by
any order or decision of the Director made without a hear-
ing, may , within thirty days after notice of the order to the
insurer or organization , make written request to the Director
for a hearing thereon . The Director shall hear such party
or parties within twenty days after receipt of such request
and shall give not less than ten days ' written notice of the
time and place of the hearing . Within fifteen days after such
hearing the Director shall affirm , reverse or modify his
previous action , specifying his reasons therefor . Pending
such hearing and decision thereon the Director may suspend
or postpone the effective date of his previous action .
( 2) Nothing contained in this Act shall require the
observance at any hearing of formal rules of pleading or
evidence .

CHAPTER 105 261
Sec . 20.-JUDICIAL REVIEW
( 1 ) Any order or decision of the Director shall be sub-
ject to review by appeal to the Franklin Circuit Court at the
instance of any party in interest, who shall , within twenty
( 20 ) days after such order or decision has become final , file
a petition for review in said Court . The petition shall state
fully the grounds upon which the review is sought , assign all
errors relied on and be verified by the petitioner , who shall
furnish copies of the petition to the respondents at the time
of filing . Summons shall issue , upon the petition , directing the
adverse party to file answer within fifteen ( 15 ) days after
service thereof, and directing the Director to certify to the
Court the complete record of the proceedings upon which
such order or decision is based .
(2 ) No new or additional evidence may be introduced
in the Circuit Court except as to fraud or misconduct of some
person engaged in the administration of this Act and affect-
ing the order or decision appealed from , but the Court shall
otherwise hear the cause upon the record as certified by the
Director . Such appeal shall not be limited to questions of
law . The Court shall determine whether the appeal shall
operate as a stay of any such order or decision of the Di-
rector . The Court may , in disposing of the issue before it ,
affirm , modify , or reverse the order or decision of the Di-
rector in whole or in part , or remand the action to the Di-
rector for further proceedings in accordance with the direc-
tion of the Court .
Sec . 21.- LAWS AMENDED AND REPEALED

vised Statutes is amended to read as follows :
" Except as provided in KRS 136.370 , 296.320 , 302.080
to 302.140 and in this Act no law of this State relating to
insurance shall apply to the exchange of indemnity contracts
as regulated by KRS 302.080 to 302.140 ."

AN ACT to regulate the making , filing and use of rates for certain
kinds , types or forms of insurance written by casualty insurance
and surety companies on risks or operations in this State , includ-
ing fidelity, surety and guaranty bonds and all other kinds , types
or forms of motor vehicle insurance ; to regulate rating organiza-
tions ; to provide fees and to provide penalties for the violation of
this Act ; to amend subsection (3 ) of Section 302.080 , Kentucky
Revised Statutes ; and to repeal Sections 296.530 to 296.680, in-
clusive , subsection ( 11 ) of Section 296.990 , Section 342.385 and sub-
section ( 2 ) of Section 342.545, Kentucky Revised Statutes and all
other laws or parts of laws in conflict or inconsistent with this Act .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1. PURPOSE OF ACT .
The purpose of this Act is to promote the public welfare
by regulating insurance rates to the end that they shall not
be excessive , inadequate or unfairly discriminatory , and to
authorize and regulate cooperative action among insurers in
rate making and in other matters within the scope of this
Act . Nothing in this Act is intended ( 1 ) to prohibit or dis-

courage reasonable competition , or (2 ) to prohibit , or en-

ly interpreted to carry into effect the provisions of this
Section .
Sec . 2. SCOPE OF ACT .
This Act applies to all kinds , types or forms of casualty
insuran on risks or operations in this State , including
fidelity surety and guaranty bonds and all other kinds , types
or forins of motor vehicle insurance , all Workmen's Com-
pensation and Employers ' Liability Insurance , except ( 1 )
accident and health insurance , ( 2 ) credit insurance , ( 3 ) title
insurance , ( 4 ) insurance against loss of or damage to , or
against liability , other than Workmen's Compensation and
Employers ' Liability , arising out of the ownership , main-
tenance or use of , any aircraft , and ( 5 ) reinsurance by one
or more insurers of all or part of another insurer's primary
insurance risks , other than joint reinsurance to the extent
stated in Section 11. With respect to such kinds , types or
forms of insurance , this Act applies to all classes of companies
including interinsurers or reciprocal underwriters regulated
by Sections 302.080 to 302.140 , inclusive , of the Kentucky Re-
vised Statutes , mutual insurers regulated by Sections 301.010
to 301.140 , inclusive , of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , and
insurers operating on the Lloyd's Plan , regulated by Sec-
tions 302.150 to 302.230 , inclusive , of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes . This Act shall not apply to assessment or coop-
crative companies organized or operating under Chapter 299
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes .
If any kind of insurance , subdivision or combination
thereof, or type of coverage subject to this Act is also sub-
ject to regulation by another regulatory Act of this State ,
an insurer to which both Acts are otherwise applicable shall
file with the Director of Insurance , hereinafter referred to
as Director , a designation as to which rate regulatory Act
shall be applicable to it with respect to such kind of insur-
ance , subdivision or combination thereof , or type or coverage .

264 ACTS OF THE GENERAL Assembly
Sec . 3. MAKING OF RATES .
( 1 ) All rates shall be made in accordance with the
following provisions :
(a ) Due consideration shall be given to past and pro-
spective loss experience within and outside this State , to ca-
tastrophe hazards , if any , to a reasonable margin for under-
writing profit and contingencies , to dividends , savings or
unabsorbed premium deposits allowed or returned by in-
surers to their policyholders , members or subscribers , to
past and prospective expenses both countrywide and those
specially applicable to this State , and to all other relevant
factors within and outside this State ;
(b) The systems of expense provisions included in the
rates for use by any insurer or group of insurers may differ
from those of other insurers or groups of insurers to reflect
the requirements of the operating methods of any such in-
surer or group with respect to any kind of insurance , or with
respect to any subdivision or combination thereof for which
subdivision or combination separate expense provisions are
applicable ;
( c) Risks may be grouped by classifications for the
establishment of rates and minimum premiums . Classifica-
tion rates may be modified to produce rates for individual
risks in accordance with rating plans which establish stand-
ards for measuring variations in hazards or expense pro-
visions , or both . Such standards may measure any differences
among risks that can be demonstrated to have a probable
effect upon losses or expenses ;
(d ) Rates shall not be excessive , inadequate or un-
fairly discriminatory .
(2) Except to the extent necessary to meet the pro-
visions of paragraph ( d ) of subsection ( 1 ) of this Section ,
uniformity among insurers in any matters within the scope of
this Section is neither required nor prohibited .
Sec . 4. RATE FILINGS .
( 1 ) Every insurer shall file with the Director every
manual of classifications , every manual of rules and rates ,
every rating plan and every modification of any of the fore-
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1948, p. 279

provisions of this Act , and requiring the discontinuance of
such act or practice .
(4 ) No insurer which makes its own filings nor any
rating organization shall support its filings by statistics or
adopt rate making recommendations , furnished to it by an
advisory organization which has not complied with this Sec-
tion or with an order of the Director involving such statistics
or recommendations issued under subsection ( 3 ) of this Sec-
tion . If the Director finds such insurer or rating organization
to be in violation of this subsection he may issue an order
requiring the discontinuance of such violation .
Sec . 11. JOINT UNDERWRITING OR JOINT RE-
INSURANCE .
( 1 ) Every group , association or other organization of
insurers which engages in joint underwriting or joint rein-
surance , shall be subject to regulation with respect thereto as
herein provided, subject , however , with respect to joint under-
writing , to all other provisions of this Act and , with respect
to joint reinsurance , to Sections 12 , 14 and 17 to 24 of this Act .

advisory organization , or group , association or other organ-
ization examined upon presentation to it of a detailed account
of such costs . The officer , manager , agents and employees of
such rating organization , advisory organization , or group ,
association or other organization may be examined at any
time under oath and shall exhibit all books , records , accounts ,
documents , or agreements governing its method of operation .
The Director shall furnish two copies of the examination
report to the organization , group or association examined
and shall notify such organization , group or association that
it may , within twenty days thereafter , request a hearing on
said report or on any facts or recommendations therein .
Before filing any such report for public inspection , the
Director shall grant a hearing to the organization , group or
association examined . The report of any such examination ,
when filed for public inspection , shall be admissible in evi-
dence in any action or proceeding brought by the Director
against the organization , group or association examined , or
its officers or agents , and shall be prima facie evidence of the
facts stated therein . The Director may withhold the report
of any such examination from public inspection for such time

Any person or organization violating any provision of
this Act shall , upon conviction , be fined not more than fifty
dollars ( $ 50 ) for each such violation , but if such violation
is found to be willfull said person or organization shall be
fined not more than five hundred dollars ( $ 500 ) for each such

alty provided by law.
Sec . 19. SUSPENSION OF LICENSE .
The Director may suspend the license of any rating
organization or insurer which fails to comply with an order
of the Director within the time limited by such order , or any
extension thereof which the Director may grant . The Director
shall not suspend the license of any rating organization or
insurer for failure to comply with an order until the time
prescribed for an appeal therefrom has expired or if an
appeal has been taken , until such order has been affirmed .
The Director may determine when a suspension of license

Sec . 20. HEARINGS , PROCEDURE .
(1 ) Any insurer or rating organization aggrieved by
any order or decision of the Director made without a hearing ,
may , within thirty days after notice of the order to the insurer
or organization , make written request to the Director for a
hearing thereon . The Director shall hear such party or parties
within twenty days after receipt of such request and shall
give not less than ten days ' written notice of the time and
place of the hearing . Within fifteen days after such hearing
the Director shall affirm , reverse or modify his previous
action , specifying his reasons therefor . Pending such hearing
and decision thereon the Director may suspend or postpone
the effective date of his previous action .
( 2 ) Nothing contained in this Act shall require the ob-
servance at any hearing of formal rules of pleading or
evidence .
Sec . 21 . JUDICIAL REVIEW .
(1 ) Any order or decision of the Director shall be sub-
ject to review by appeal to the Franklin Circuit Court at the
instance of any party in interest, who shall , within twenty
days after such order or decision has become final , file a
petition for review in said court . The petition shall state fully
the grounds upon which a review is sought , assign all errors
relied on and be verified by the petitioner , who shall furnish
copies of the petition to the respondents at the time of filing .
Summons shall issue , upon the petition , directing the adverse
party to file answer within fifteen days after service thereof ,
and directing the Director to certify to the court the complete
record of the proceedings upon which such order or decision
is based .
( 2 ) No new or additional evidence may be introduced
in the Circuit Court except as to fraud or misconduct of
some person engaged in the administration of this Act and
affecting the order or decision appealed from , but the court
shall otherwise hear the cause upon the record as certified by

CHAPTER 107
(H. B. 402 )
AN ACT pertaining to child labor and employment and regulation of
employment of infants .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1. As used in this Act , " gainful occupation " does
not include employment in farm work or in domestic service
in a private home , nor occasional employment by a house-
holder in connection with the household and not in connection
with the householder's business or occupation , such as grass
cutting or carrying ashes or similar casual domestic tasks ,
nor the delivery of newspapers on regularly scheduled routes .
Sec . 2. No minor under fourteen years of age shall be
employed , permitted , or suffered to work in, about or in
connection with any gainful occupation at any time .
Sec . 3. A minor who has passed his fourteenth birthday
but is under sixteen years of age may be employed , permitted
or suffered to work in , about or inconnection with any gainful
occupation , except :
(1 ) He may not be employed during school hours unless
he has been found incapable of profiting by further instruc-
tion, as provided in Section 9 of this Act , and then only in
such occupations as are authorized in accordance with that
section , or unless he has graduated from high school .
( 2 ) He may not be employed :
(a) In , about or in connection with any manufacturing
or mechanical establishment .
(b) On or in connection with any power-driven machin-
ery except under school supervision as provided in Section 22 .
(c) As a messenger for any person , firm or corporation
engaged primarily in the business of transmitting or deliver-
ing goods or messages .
(d ) In the manufacture , transportation , or use of ex-
plosives or highly inflammable substances ; in or about mines
or quarries ; in any tunnel or excavation ; in logging opera-
tions or in any sawmill , lath mill , shingle mill , or cooperage

classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for child labor in Kentucky. It defines 'gainful occupation' and sets age restrictions for employment. Minors under 14 are prohibited from any gainful occupation. Minors between 14 and 16 have restrictions on when and where they can be employed, including limitations on working during school hours, in manufacturing or mechanical establishments, with power-driven machinery, as messengers, or in hazardous occupations like those involving explosives or mining.

Sec . 4. A minor who has passed his sixteenth birthday
but is under eighteen may be employed , permitted , or suffered
to work in , about or in connection with any gainful occupation ,
except the occupations enumerated or described in para-
graphs ( d) , ( e ) and ( f ) of subsection ( 2 ) of Section 3 of this
Act, and except that a female minor of such age may not be
employed in the distribution or sale of newspapers , magazines
or periodicals , or in the street occupations of peddling or
bootblacking or in any other occupations pursued in any
street or public place , nor as a messenger for any person ,
firm or corporation engaged primarily in the business of
transmitting or delivering goods or messages .
Sec . 5. No person shall furnish or sell to any minor
any article of any description with the knowledge that the
minor intends to sell said article in violation of this Act . No
person shall continue to furnish or sell articles of any descrip-
tion to a minor after having received written notice from any
officer charged with the enforcement of this Act , or from the
officer issuing employment certificates , that the minor is not
permitted to sell such articles .
Sec . 6. ( 1 ) No minor under 18 years of age shall be
employed , permitted or suffered to work in any gainful occu-
pation more than six consecutive days in any one week , nor
more than eight hours in any one day , nor more than forty
hours in any week , except that the six -day limitation shall not
apply to employment as a newsboy in the sale or delivery
of newspapers .
( 2 ) No minor under sixteen years of age who is attend-
ing school and working outside of school hours shall be em-
ployed , permitted or suffered to work outside of school hours
for more than three hours on a school day or more than
twenty -three hours during a school week .
(3) No minor of sixteen or seventeen years of age who
is attending school in session during the day other than a
part -time or continuation school , and working outside of
school hours , shall be employed , permitted or suffered to
work for more than four hours on a school day or more than
twenty -eight hours during a school week .

missioner of Industrial Relations and the Superintendent of

tificate shall not authorize the employment of a minor in or

(2 ) If on account of mental condition a minor has been
exempted from attendance at school or recommended by a
state child guidance clinic or other approved examiner to be
placed in an institution for retarded children, the certificating
officer shall issue an employment certificate to such minor
only when satisfied that the work available and the super-
vision under which it will be performed are such that the job
is suitable and safe for said minor to undertake .
Sec . 10. The employment certificates required by this
Act shall be issued by the local Board of Education through
its superintendent or other authorized agent , in such form
and under such conditions as may be prescribed by the
Department of Education .
Sec . 11. The Department of Education shall prescribe
such rules and regulations for the issuance of employment
certificates and age certificates as will promote uniformity
and efficiency in the administration of this Act . It shall also
supply to local issuing officers all blank forms to be used in
connection with the issuance of such certificates . Duplicates
of each employment or age certificate shall be mailed by the
issuing officer to the Department of Industrial Relations
within one week after issuance .
Sec . 12. A person designated to issue employment cer-
tificates may refuse to grant such a certificate if in his judg-
ment the best interests of the minor would be served by such
refusal .

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Sections 331a-1 to 331a-17, inclusive, of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, Relating to Child Labor
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes concerning child labor. It outlines requirements for issuing employment certificates, including special provisions for minors deemed incapable of profiting from further instruction or those with mental conditions. It specifies that special employment certificates do not authorize factory work and that the Department of Education will prescribe rules for certificate issuance. Local Boards of Education are responsible for issuing certificates through authorized agents, and duplicates are to be sent to the Department of Industrial Relations. Issuing officers have discretion to refuse certificates if it's in the minor's best interest.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text focuses on the regulation of child labor and the issuance of employment certificates. It details procedures and conditions for minors to obtain these certificates, including provisions for those with special needs or learning difficulties. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general labor law concerning the employment of minors. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 107 291
(1 ) The board of education of the district in which the
child resides shall exempt from the requirement of attendance
upon a regular public day school every child of compulsory
school age :
(a ) Who is a graduate from an accredited or an ap-
proved four -year high school ; or
(b) Who is enrolled and in regular attendance in a
private or parochial regular day school approved by the State
Board of Education ; or
(c) Whose physical or mental condition prevents or
renders inadvisable attendance at school or application to
study ; or
(d ) Who is deaf or blind to an extent that renders him
incapable of receiving instruction in the regular elementary
or secondary schools , but whose mental condition permits
application to study .
(2 ) Before granting an exemption under paragraph ( c )
of subsection ( 1 ) of this section the board of education shall
require satisfactory evidence , in the form of a signed state-
ment of a licensed physician or public health officer , that the
condition of the child prevents or renders inadvisable attend-
ance at school or application to study . On the basis of such
evidence the board may exclude any such child from school .
Sec . 28. ( 1 ) Minors 16 and 17 years of age who have
been lawfully employed for a period of at least ninety days
prior to the effective date of this Act shall be exempt from
the provisions of this Act .
( 2 ) Minors 14 and 15 years of age who have been
lawfully employed during school hours for a period of at
least ninety days prior to the effective date of this Act , may
continue to be so employed subject to all other provisions of
this Act , notwithstanding the provisions of subsection ( 1 ) of
Section 3.
Approved March 23 , 1948 .

292 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 108
(H. B. 411 )
AN ACT to amend Sections 241.160 and 241.170 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes , relating to the appointment and qualification of City
Alcoholic Beverage Administrators .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1. Section 241.160 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The legislative body of any city of the first , second , third
or fourth class in which traffic in alcoholic beverages is not
forbidden by KRS Chapter 242 shall by ordinance create the
office of City Alcoholic Beverage Control Administrator , or
shall assign the duties of this office to a presently established
city office .
Sec . 2. Section 241.170 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
(1 ) The city administrator in each city of the first class ,
and such investigators and clerks as are deemed necessary
for the proper conduct of his office , shall be appointed by the
mayor. The city administrator in each city of the first class ,
and his investigators , shall have full police powers of peace
officers , and their jurisdiction shall be co -extensive with such
city. They may inspect any premises where alcoholic bev-
erages are manufactured , sold , stored or otherwise trafficked
in, without first obtaining a search warrant .
(2 ) The city administrator in each city of the second ,
third or fourth class shall be appointed by the city manager
if there is one , if there is no city manager , the city adminis-
trator shall be appointed by the mayor.
(3) No person shall be a city administrator , an investi-
gator or an employee of the city under the supervision of the
city administrator , who would be disqualified to be a member
of the Board under KRS 241.100 .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

CHAPTER 109
(H. B. 430)
AN ACT to amend Sections 69.070, Kentucky Revised Statutes, so as to
provide an additional Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney in coun-
ties having a population of 200,000 or more .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
(1. ) Kentucky Revised Statutes , Section 69.070 is here-
by amended to read as follows : Appointment and salary of
Assistant Commonwealth's Attorneys in counties having a
population of 200,000 or more . In counties having a popula-
tion of 200,000 or more , the Commonwealth's Attorney shall
appoint a first , second and third Assistant Commonwealth's
Attorney for terms of four years each, but they may be
removed at any time by the Commonwealth's Attorney . Each
Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney shall receive an annual
salary of $4,000.00 , payable out of the county levy in equal
monthly installments .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

CHAPTER 112
(H. B. 447)
AN ACT relating to education in fields related to health , with par-
ticular reference to the education of , and the training of nurses
and doctors of the Negro race.
WHEREAS , There is no accredited public , or private , agency in Ken-
tucky where Negro women may obtain training in the profession of
nursing , nor are facilities for postgraduate training available to
Negro doctors , and
WHEREAS , there are adequate provisions for such training for mem-
bers of other races within the existing facilities of accredited hos-
pitals which accept Negro patients .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
The provisions of KRS 158.020 shall not be construed
to prohibit the giving of instruction in nursing , medicine ,
surgery , or other related courses of graduate grade or on
the professional level , within any hospital , if the governing
body of the hospital , by a majority vote of its members , so
elects .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

Section 1 .
Section 220.530 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended and re -enacted to read as follows :
220.530 (2062g -54 ) Annexation of District and Part of
District by City.
( 1 ) Where a city annexes an entire sanitation district
organized under this Chapter , the city shall be liable from and
after the date of such annexation for all of the debts and
liabilities of such district , including , but not by way of limita-
tion , all revenue bonds and all debts and liabilities secured by
revenues of the district , and all other bonded and floating debt
of the district , and such debts and liabilities shall be payable
out of the general funds of the city , and such city shall be the
owner of all the property and rights of the district , and the
users of sewers in the district shall be relieved from any
further rentals and obligations , except that if the territory
in the district becomes part of the territory in a metropolitan
sewer district formed under Chapter 76 of the Kentucky Re-
vised Statutes , such users shall pay the regular charges of
such metropolitan sewer district , and the sanitation district
shall thereupon be automatically dissolved .
( 2 ) If only a portion of the sanitation district is an-
nexed , the city shall be the owner of all the sewers within the
annexed portion , and shall be liable from and after the date
of such annexation for a proportionate part of the debts and
liabilities of the district , as same are enumerated above in
Subsection ( 1 ) such part to be determined by the ratio that

classification.title: AN ACT relating to sanitation districts and the annexation thereof by cities.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 220.530 concerning the annexation of sanitation districts by cities. It clarifies the liabilities and ownership of property when a city annexes an entire district or a portion of one. If a city annexes an entire district, it assumes all debts and liabilities, and users are relieved of further rentals unless the territory becomes part of a metropolitan sewer district. If only a portion is annexed, the city owns the sewers within that portion and is liable for a proportionate share of the district's debts.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative and financial aspects of sanitation districts and their annexation by cities. It does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it relate to any of the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws (education, housing, public accommodations, etc.). CHAPTER 120
(H. B. 471 )
AN ACT relating to the working of prisoners outside of the walls of the
prison ; amending KRS 197.140 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 197.140 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
No prisoner who has been sentenced for rape , attempted
rape , detaining a woman against her will , or who has escaped
or attempted to escape , or who has been convicted and sen-
tenced more than twice , or who has been convicted of highway
robbery or bank robbery , shall be worked or released for work
outside of the walls of the prison . No prisoner who has been
sentenced to life imprisonment shall be worked or released
for work outside of the walls of the prison until he has
actually served within the walls of the prison for at least
one year of his sentence .
Approved March 26 , 1948

wealth of Kentucky :

(1 ) The board of supervisors of each city of the third
class shall convene in the council hall on any date during the
year in which property is listed by owners for taxation pur-
poses , or on the first Monday in February in the next succeed-
ing year , or at some later date not later than the first Monday
in April . Upon convening , the board shall proceed to the
discharge of its duties . The assessor shall meet with the board
and act as its clerk . The board shall supervise all assessments ,

312 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 123
(H. B. 484 )
AN ACT relating to high school service for war veterans .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Each school district in this state shall admit to its twelve-
grade school service , without tuition , any veteran of the
Armed Forces whose attendance was interrupted , before com-
pleting the approved twelve -grade school course , by reason
of induction or enlistment in the Armed Forces , who applies
for re -enrollment in the public school system of the district
of his residence not later than four years after his honorable
discharge from the Armed Forces . However , this is not in-
tended to apply to enrollment by veterans in special courses
for whom tuition is paid under the provisions of Federal laws ,
or otherwise .
Approved March 25 , 1948 .

(H. B. 486 )

Auditor of Public Accounts , Secretary of State , Statute Revision
Commission , Department of Treasury , Department of Finance ,
Department of Welfare , Department of Education , University of
Kentucky , Lincoln Institute , The Military Department , and declaring
the existence of an emergency .

wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1 .
There is hereby appropriated out of the General Ex-
penditure Fund in the State Treasury as of the effective date
of this Act the following sums to the offices , departments ,
commissions and institutions , and divisions of the State Gov-
ernment for the purpose of supplying deficiencies in the
appropriations heretofore made for the fiscal year 1947-48 .

CHAPTER 126
(H. B. 488 )
AN ACT relating to the licensing of motor vehicles owned by govern-
mental units , amending Section 186.060 and Section 186.240 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
1 . Section 186.060 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows : Applications for registration for
motor vehicles owned exclusively by a county , city , or board
of education in the state , or by the state or federal govern-
ment , shall be accompanied by a statement from the head of
a department of the governmental unit that owns the motor
vehicle certifying that the motor vehicle is exclusively owned
and operated by the governmental unit . The application and
statement shall be forwarded by the county clerk to the de-
partment , which shall give special authority to the clerk to
register it . Upon receiving that authority , the clerk shall issue
a registration receipt and the official number plate described
in Section 186.240 , Sub -section ( 3 ) , as amended by this Act,
and report the registration to the head of the department
authorizing the registration . For his services in issuing such
certificate of registration and number plate and reporting the
same , the county clerk shall be entitled to a fee of fifty cents
in each instance , to be paid by the department upon whose
authorization such license was issued ,
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1948, p. 332

(2 ) Keep a numerical record of all registration numbers

plished , and declaring an emergency . This Act shall be known and
cited as the Moss-Hibbitts Act .
WHEREAS , as a result of an explosion in the Straight Creek Coal Mine
in Bell County , Kentucky , on the 26th day of December, 1945 , twenty
or more men were killed and their bodies entombed in the mine ; and
WHEREAS , efforts to recover such bodies have been unsuccessful , due to
lack of funds and proper equipment on the part of the relatives of
the deceased men and other interested persons , and due to the
exceptional obstacles that confront such removal efforts , and it
appears that recovery may never be accomplished through private
efforts ; and
WHEREAS , the fact that the bodies of those revered citizens of Kentucky
remain entombed at the scene of that great disaster is a reflection
upon the good name and credit of the Commonwealth , and it is in
the best interests and welfare of the people of the Commonwealth
that public funds and facilities be made available for the removal
and suitable burial of such bodies , and particularly because there
exist some elements of moral responsibility on the part of the Com-
monwealth in connection with the disaster ;
NOW THEREFORE
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. ( 1 ) There is hereby appropriated to the
Department of Mines and Minerals , out of any unappropriat-
ed funds in the State Treasury as of the effective date hereof,
such sum , not to exceed $ 100,000.00 , as may be required to
provide for the removal and suitable burial of the bodies of
those men who were killed in the Straight Creek Coal Mine
disaster in Bell County , Kentucky , on or about December 26 ,
1945 , and whose bodies remain entombed in the mine .
( 2 ) The Department of Mines and Minerals shall de-
termine the most practical method of removing the bodies
from the mine , whether by entering the old mine for that
purpose or by driving openings through the coal adjoining
or adjacent to the old mine to a point where the old mine can
be entered for the purpose of removing the bodies , and the
department in selecting the most practical method shall con-
sider the safety of the men employed in said work . If the
department selects the method of driving openings through
the coal adjoining and adjacent to the old mine , it shall enter
into such contracts , with the owner or owners of the surface
and mineral rights adjoining and adjacent to the old mine , as

CHAPTER 128
( S. B. 10 )
AN ACT RELATING TO PAYMENT OF TAXES IN CITIES ,. and
declaring an emergency .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Subsection ( 2 ) of Section 92.590 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(2) In cities of the second class , taxpayers owing ad
valorem taxes shall pay them , without demand therefor , at
the treasurer's office . The taxpayer may pay the whole tax
at any time after the tax bills have been made out and deliv-
ered to him . The city legislative body may provide by or-
dinance that taxes paid during the month of April in the year
in which they are due and payable may be reduced by a
discount of three percent , and taxes paid during the month
of May in that year may be reduced by a discount of two
percent . Taxes not paid within one calendar month after they
are due shall be deemed delinquent and shall have added to
them a penalty of ten percent , and shall thereafter bear
interest at the rate of one-half of one percent for each month
or fraction of a month until paid , or until the property of
the delinquent has been sold for the tax . When taxes are paid
the treasurer shall note on the stub of the bill the amounts
paid and the date thereof, and shall deliver the receipted bill ,
countersigned by the auditor , to the taxpayer . When any
person desires to pay taxes on any one item of real estate
in a tax bill he shall be allowed to do so , if the city's interest
will not be prejudiced thereby , and shall be given a receipt
showing the date and the amount of the payment , the number
of the tax bill , the name of the person against whom the tax
bill is made out and a sufficient description to identify the
property on which the taxes are paid . A corresponding nota-
tion shall be made on the tax bill and the stub , and the city's
lien shall thereupon stand released as to that property , but
the tax bill shall not be surrendered until paid in full .
Whereas cities of the state face a crisis because of their
need for revenue and whereas this act will provide relief for

CHAPTER 129
( S. B. 11 )
AN ACT authorizing retirement and disability plans for employes of
cities, counties and their agencies .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Cities of all classes and counties and the
agencies of cities and counties are authorized to establish and
operate plans for the payment of retirement , disability or
hospitalization benefits to their employes . Such a plan may
require employes to pay a percentage of their salaries into a
fund from which benefits are paid , or the city , county or
agency may pay out of its own funds the entire cost of the
benefits . A plan may include a combination of contributions
by employes and by the city, county or agency into a fund
from which benefits are paid, or it may take any form desired
by the city , county or agency . Each city , county or agency
may make rules and regulations and do all other things neces-
sary in the establishment and operation of such a plan . The
term agency as used herein shall include boards appointed to
operate water works , electric plants , hospitals , airports ,
housing projects , golf courses , parks , health departments , or
any other public project .
Section 2. In the event the Congress of the United States
extends the Social Security Act to cover employes of cities ,
counties and their agencies , such cities , counties and their
agencies are authorized to participate in the United States
Social Security program by doing any and all things neces-
sary to provide their employes with retirement or disability
benefits or any other benefits that may be provided by that
program .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1948, p. 339

CHAPTER 130
( S. B. 12 )
AN ACT to amend section 92.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes ,
relating to the fiscal year of cities other than the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 92.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
The fiscal year of cities other than the first class may
begin on the following dates :
(1) In cities of the second class , January 1 , June 1 or
July 1.
(2 ) In cities of the third class , January 1 , June 1 or
July 1.
(3) In cities of the fourth , fifth and sixth classes , Jan-
uary 1, June 1 or July 1 .
Any city desiring to change the date of the beginning of
its fiscal year may operate for the portion of any year
necessary in making the change .
Not approved .
Received by Secretary of State March 1, 1948 .

CHAPTER 132
(S. B. 24)
AN ACT appropriating money out of the General Expenditure Fund in
the State Treasury for the current fiscal year , for the purpose of
supplementing the salaries of teachers in common schools .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. There is hereby appropriated out of the unappro-
priated surplus in the General Expenditure Fund in the State
Treasury as of the effective date of this Act, the sum of
$3,000,000.00 for the purpose of supplementing the salaries
of teachers employed in the public elementary and secondary
schools of Kentucky for the school year beginning July 1,
1947 and ending June 30 , 1948. The sum so appropriated
shall be distributed to each school district by the Superin-
tendent of Public Instruction as a supplement to the Common
School Per Capita Fund appropriated in paragraph ( e ) of
Item 18 of Part One of Chapter 38 of the Acts of 1946 , the
distribution to be in accordance with the law governing dis-
tribution of the Common School Per Capita Fund.

Agriculture , Labor and Statistics , with particular relation to the
